metadata.title: Write a Five-Artifact Software Design Document with Claude Code

beats.narration_text: This is Musinique. Three hours of drift because a local-only editor became a multi-user backend. One eleven-minute SDD prevents it.

props.command: Write a Five-Artifact Software Design Document with Claude Code
props.segment: Write a Five-Artifact Software Design Document with Claude Code

beats.narration_text: The SDD is the decision that prevents you from making the same decision twice under deadline pressure. The Principle Collision Test is what separates a real principle from a tagline.

Prompt design is the skill the platform doesn't want you to have, because a musician who can write a precise prompt is a musician who doesn't need to buy someone else's pre-packaged workflow. Every prompt in this video is a template you can adapt — paste it, swap the specifics, run it on your own catalog. The instruction is the tool.

artifactLines: The same tools the platforms use — pointed back at you.
Roll your own. Own the deck.

beats.narration_text: We give Claude a v0 sentence and ask it to generate a complete five-section SDD, then run the Principle Collision Test on all three architecture principles.

props.command:
claude "v0: Local timeline editor — one user, browser-only, no backend.
Generate a five-section SDD:
  1. Problem Statement (one user, one done-condition)
  2. Architecture Principles (run Collision Test)
  3. User Needs (stopwatch-measurable)
  4. Component List (explicit non-components)
  5. Open Questions
Flag any principle that survives no realistic conflict."

beats.narration_text: Claude generates the five sections. The Collision Test flags a conflict between local-only and any future sync requirement. The non-component list explicitly names: no backend, no database, no login.

props.code:
# SDD — Local Timeline Editor

## 1. Problem Statement
One user edits a personal timeline in the browser.
Done when: user can add, reorder, and delete events
without losing data across page refreshes.

## 2. Architecture Principles
- Local-only: no data leaves the browser [COLLISION TEST: conflicts with sync]
- Persistence via localStorage only [COLLISION TEST: conflicts with multi-device]
- No external dependencies [COLLISION TEST: conflicts with rich UI libs]

## 4. Component List
IN: EventList, EventEditor, LocalStorageAdapter
OUT (non-components): backend, database, login, sync, sharing
props.title: sdd.md

beats.narration_text: Five sections: Problem Statement names one user and one done-condition. Architecture Principles survive the Collision Test. User Needs are stopwatch-measurable. Component List names no backend as an explicit non-component.
We add multi-user sync to scope and re-run. Claude flags the immediate conflict with the local-only principle rather than silently resolving it.

props.command: claude "Add 'multi-user sync' to scope in the SDD.\nRe-run Principle Collision Test.\nDoes local-only survive? Flag the conflict explicitly."

beats.narration_text: The Collision Test fires immediately: local-only versus sync is an irreconcilable conflict at the principle level. Claude flags it and asks for a scope decision rather than inventing a workaround. The SDD caught the drift before the first line of code.
The SDD is not documentation after the fact — it is the decision itself, made once, in writing, before any code is written. The Collision Test is what keeps principles from being taglines.
Next: score a build plan using the Boondoggle Score.
Musinique. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At nikbearbrown.com.
